content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · · Administration · · · Executive/Legal · · · Enforcement · · · Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660            322-5901           322-6441           322-6444

March 5, 1980


Daniel S. Hentschke
Assistant City Attorney
City of Carlsbad
1200 Elm Avenue
Carlsbad, CA 92003

Dear Mr. Hentschke:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act on behalf of Eric Larson, Planning
Commissioner for the City of Carlsbad. I apologize for the
delay in this response. The following informal advice is
provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).1/
The facts as I understand them from our telephone conversa-
tions and from your letter are as follows:

     Mr. Larson is the manager of one of three divisions of
a closely-held corporation and is answerable directly to the
president of the corporation, who is also the majority share-
holder and who makes all business operation decisions for the
corporation. As a member of the Planning Commission,
Mr. Larson is now confronted with decisions concerning the
implementation of a redevelopment plan which would concededly
have a foreseeable material financial effect on four conti-
guous parcels of real property owned by the corporation
president in his personal capacity. You have asked whether
the Political Reform Act therefore prohibits Mr. Larson from
participating in these decisions.

     Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Act prohibit a public
official from making, participating in making or otherwise
using his official position to influence a governmental
decision which will have a foreseeable material financial
effect, distinguishable from the decision's effects on the
public generally, on the various financial interests of the
official, including business entities which employ him or

_________________________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise specified.

Daniel S. Hentschke
March 5, 1980
Page Two


are sources of income to him. As the real property which will
be affected by the decisions before Mr. Larson are not a corporate asset but are held by the majority shareholder/president
in his personal capacity, the question becomes whether the
corporation and its majority shareholder have an identity of
financial interest under the provisions of the Political
Reform Act.

     This question has not previously been raised in the
context of conflicts of interest, but the Commission has considered it in the context of reporting campaign contributions
under Chapter 4 of the Act. In the opinion requested in
Thomas G. Lumsdon, 2 FPPC Opinions 140 (No. 75-205, Sept. 7,
1976), the Commission held that contributions by a closelyheld corporation and its majority shareholder should be
aggregated to determine if any filing obligation was thereby
incurred. In reaching this conclusion, the Commission noted:

     By definition, a majority shareholder exercises
almost complete control over the activities of a
closely held corporation. He, for all practical purposes, appoints the board of directors and the officers
of the company and they are subject to his ultimate
direction in arriving at decisions. If they are not
responsive to his desires, he can easily replace them.
Accordingly, corporate action generally reflects the
judgment and beliefs of the majority shareholder.

                         2 FPPC Opinions at 143.

     This is even more true when the majority shareholder of a
closely-held corporation also is the president of the
corporation.

     The conclusion in Lumsdon was consistent with treatment
of closely-held corporations in other legal contexts. For
example, under certain conditions, earnings and losses of a
closely-held corporation can be treated for tax purposes as
earnings and losses of its individual shareholders. See
Subchapter S of the Internal Revenue Code, Sections 1371-1378.
The inseparable nature of an individual and the incorporated
business of which he was the sole shareholder was cited as
the basis for allowing the individual to receive unemployment
benefits in Cooperman v. Unemployment Insurance Appeals Board,
49 Cal. App. 3d 1 (1975). Finally, the Supreme Court of the
United States has held that "the corporate form may be disregarded in the interests of justice when it is used to defeat
an overriding public policy." Bangor Punta Operations, Inc.
v. Bangor & Aroostock R. Co., 417 U.S. 703 (1974).

Daniel S. Hentschke
March 5, 1980
Page Three


     The Act specifically identifies a public official's
"source of income" as among the interests which may give rise
to a prohibited conflict of interest.  Section 87103(c).
While "income" is extensively defined in the Act, Section
82030, what constitutes a "source" of it is not.  It is clear
at least that Mr. Larson's employment is a "source of income"
to him.  In keeping with the purposes of the Act, we conclude
that in this case the president/majority shareholder of the
corporation for which Mr. Larson works may also be considered
a source of income to Mr. Larson.  Although for other purposes
the corporation would be considered Mr. Larson's source of
income, there can be no question that in a closely-held cor-
poration situation such as here the president/majority share-
holder of a corporation effectively controls the employment
relationship itself.  Accordingly, we conclude that the
majority shareholder is a source of income to Mr. Larson and
that he should therefore disqualify himself from any decision
which would have a material financial effect on the shareholder.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Sarah T. Cameron
                                        Deputy Chief for
                                           Conflicts of Interests
                                        Legal Division

STC:nrw

CITY OF CARLSBAD
1200 ELM AVENUE
CARLSBAD, CALIFORNIA 92008
(714) 729-1181

VINCENT F. BIONDO, JR.
CITY ATTORNEY

November 13, 1979

Sarah Cameron
Deputy Director
Legal/Conflicts Division
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA  95804

Dear Sarah:

I'm sending this letter as a follow-up to the telephone conversation
we had a few weeks back.  As you will recall, the issue we discussed
at that time was whether or not a Planning Commissioner, who
was an employee of a closed corporation, might have a potential
conflict of interest when a decision could foreseeably have a
material financial effect on property owned by the majority share-
holder and president of that closed corporation.  I am requesting
informal written advice on the matter.

Briefly, the facts of the case are these:  Eric Larson is a Planning
Commissioner for the City of Carlsbad and is employed by a privately
held corporation.  The majority shareholder of that corporation
owns four contiguous parcels of real property within the City's
redevelopment area.  These properties are held individually by the
corporation president and are not a corporate asset.  The owner of
the property is the controlling officer and president of the
corporation.  He makes all of the decisions with regard to the
business operation of the corporation.  The corporation is involved in
the growing and selling of potted and other plants.  There are
three divisions in the corporation and a manager for each of the
three divisions.  Mr. Larson is one of the managers and is generally
in charge of the potted plant operation.  As manager Mr. Larson
answers directly to the president of the corporation.  Implementation
of the City's redevelopment plan could have a substantial impact
on the four contiguous properties owned by the corporation president.
Consistent with the requirements of the Political Reform Act the
City of Carlsbad has adopted a Local Conflict of Interest Code.
Section 500 of that Code states in part:

"It shall be the duty of each designated employee
[including Planning Commissioners] to disqualify
himself or herself from making, or participating
in the making of City decisions...if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from the decision's effect on the
public generally, on:

Sarah Cameron                    -2-                    November 15, 1979

     (a)  Any business entity in which the designated
          employee has a direct or indirect investment
          which is reportable;
                         . . . .
     (c)  Any source of reportable income...aggregating
          two hundred fifty dollars or more...;

     (d)  Any business entity in which the designated
          employee is a director, officer, partner,
          trustee, employee, or holds any position
          of management."

     Because the property is not a corporate asset, it would appear
that subdivisions (a) and (d) are not applicable and that dis-
qualification must be based solely on the possibility of an effect
on a source of reportable income.

     In our telephone discussions it was decided that the conclusion
regarding disqualification would depend upon whether it was
reasonable to identify the property owner with the corporation in
such a manner to find that the property owner was in effect the
source of income. Apparently the issue of a majority shareholder's
relationship to a closed corporation has never been decided in the
context of a conflict of interest decision. Decision 75-205 of
the Fair Political Practices Commission, contained in 2 FPPC Ops.
140, at 143, indicates an analysis which may be applied to determin-
ing whether a majority shareholder can be identified with the
corporation itself for the purposes of campaign contributions. A
similar analysis may be applicable in this case. In addition, the
case of Witt v. Morrow, 70 Cal.App.3d 821, 139 Cal.Rptr. 165
(1977) may be applicable in this case. In the final paragraph of
that decision, the Court notes:

     "Any employee in the private or public sector
     wishes to keep his job and maintain good relations
     with his employer. A person who must make decisions
     which may affect his employer's purse, is in a
     situation where he may not give full consideration
     to the merits of the decision."

     Based on our telephone discussions and these opinions, it appears
that a decision on the redevelopment project, which could have
an effect on the value of the four parcels of property, could
reasonably be found to have a foreseeable material financial
effect on the commissioner's source of income through the president

Sarah Cameron                    -3-                    November 15, 1979


of the corporation; even though the actual source of income is the
corporation and not the majority shareholder or one of the cor-
poration's officers.

     On the other hand, because the actual source of income is the
corporation and not the property owner, it would be technically
correct to find that a decision effecting the value of the
property would have no effect on the source of income because of
the separate legal identity of the corporation and its share-
holders. Further, the effect on the value of the property would
have no effect on the assets of the corporation itself and, thus,
could be said to have no effect on the source of income.

     The whole issue of conflict of interest and disqualification has
recently become a source of major concern among officials in the
City of Carlsbad. Part of this is in reaction to a number of
resignations by individuals who choose to forego participation in
public affairs rather than to disclose financial interests. While
a majority of the public officials and employees understand the
purpose and the intent of the law and agree fully with its objectives,
they question the propriety of disqualifying an individual, such
as Commissioner Larson, whom they hold in the highest esteem, for
what they see to be a rather slight conflict of interest.

     Your assistance in resolving this matter is greatly appreciated.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        VINCENT F. BIONDY, JR., City Attorney

                                        By [illegible]
                                           DANIEL S. HENTSCHKE
                                           Assistant City Attorney

DSH/mla

cc:  Planning Director
     Housing and Redevelopment Director

sections.question: Whether the Political Reform Act prohibits Eric Larson, a Planning Commissioner for the City of Carlsbad who is employed by a closely-held corporation, from participating in decisions concerning implementation of a redevelopment plan that would have a foreseeable material financial effect on four contiguous parcels of real property owned by the corporation's majority shareholder/president in his personal capacity.
sections.conclusion: The majority shareholder is a source of income to Mr. Larson and he should therefore disqualify himself from any decision which would have a material financial effect on the shareholder. The identity of financial interest between a closely-held corporation and its majority shareholder/president should be recognized under the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions, consistent with how such identity is treated in campaign contribution reporting.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a Planning Commissioner employed by a closely-held corporation have a conflict of interest when voting on matters that would affect property owned by the corporation's majority shareholder in his personal capacity?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that in a closely-held corporation where the president/majority shareholder effectively controls the employment relationship, the shareholder should be considered a source of income to the employee, requiring disqualification from decisions affecting that shareholder's financial interests.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated November 13, 1979) and the FPPC response letter (dated March 5, 1980). Both sections clearly present the question and conclusion. Response cites FPPC Opinion No. 75-205 and relevant statutory sections. Well-structured advice letter with detailed legal analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Political Reform Act prohibits Eric Larson, a Planning Commissioner for the City of Carlsbad who is employed by a closely-held corporation, from participating in decisions concerning implementation of a redevelopment plan that would have a foreseeable material financial effect on four contiguous parcels of real property owned by the corporation's majority shareholder/president in his personal capacity.

CONCLUSION: The majority shareholder is a source of income to Mr. Larson and he should therefore disqualify himself from any decision which would have a material financial effect on the shareholder. The identity of financial interest between a closely-held corporation and its majority shareholder/president should be recognized under the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions, consistent with how such identity is treated in campaign contribution reporting.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to a City of Carlsbad Assistant City Attorney's request regarding whether a Planning Commissioner employed by a closely-held corporation must disqualify himself from decisions affecting property owned by the corporation's majority shareholder. The FPPC concludes that the majority shareholder should be treated as a source of income, requiring disqualification.
